text: WHO Syndromic Management of Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs)

The syndromic approach to STI management is recommended for resource-limited settings where laboratory diagnosis is unavailable. This approach groups similar clinical presentations into syndromes and provides empiric antimicrobial therapy covering the most common causative organisms.

Key syndromes include: urethritis/cervicitis, vaginal discharge, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), genital ulcer disease (GUD), and inguinal lymphadenopathy (buboes). Early detection and treatment prevents complications including ectopic pregnancy, infertility, and increased HIV transmission.

Vaginal discharge is the most common STI presentation in women. Causes include Neisseria gonorrhoeae (gonorrhea), Chlamydia trachomatis, Trichomonas vaginalis, and Candida albicans. Bacterial vaginosis (BV) alters normal flora but is not sexually transmitted.

items: Ask about: duration of discharge, color (yellow/green/gray/white), odor, itching, pelvic pain, dysuria, dyspareunia
Examine: cervix (erythema, mucopurulent cervical exudate, cervical bleeding), vagina (erythema, excoriations), bimanual exam (adnexal tenderness for PID)
Yellow/green mucopurulent cervical discharge suggests gonorrhea or chlamydia
Gray/white homogeneous discharge with fishy odor suggests bacterial vaginosis
Profuse frothy discharge with vulvar erythema suggests trichomoniasis

caption: Treatment Regimens for Vaginal Discharge Syndrome
rows: Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM once + Azithromycin 1 g PO once
Cefixime 400 mg PO once + Azithromycin 1 g PO once
Metronidazole 500 mg PO BD x 7 days
Clindamycin 300 mg PO BD x 7 days

text: Urethritis in men presents with dysuria and urethral discharge. Cervicitis in women presents with cervical erythema and mucopurulent cervical exudate. Main causes: Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. Non-gonococcal urethritis (NGU) is usually chlamydial or ureaplasmal.

items: Ask about: dysuria, urethral discharge, testicular pain, scrotal swelling, recent unprotected sex
Examine: urethral discharge quality (purulent/mucopurulent vs clear/mucoid), testicles for tenderness/swelling, inguinal lymph nodes
Mucopurulent or clear discharge suggests chlamydia or NGU

caption: Treatment for Urethritis/Cervicitis Syndrome
rows: Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM once + Azithromycin 1 g PO once OR Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM once + Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD x 7 days
Spectinomycin 2 g IM once + Azithromycin 1 g PO once
Azithromycin 1 g PO single dose OR Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD x 7 days

text: PID is infection of the upper reproductive tract (fallopian tubes, ovaries, endometrium). Caused by ascending infection from lower genital tract, usually Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis, or anaerobes. Risk factors: multiple partners, inconsistent condom use, history of STI, young age.

No single finding has 100% sensitivity. Empiric treatment recommended if ANY of the following present in woman with pelvic/lower abdominal pain: pelvic tenderness (uterine, adnexal, or cervical motion tenderness), evidence of STI (discharge, positive test), fever (>38.3°C), elevated ESR or CRP, or new/purulent cervical mucopurulent cervical discharge/cervical friability.

items: Mild-moderate PID: outpatient treatment, no fever or toxicity, mild symptoms
Severe PID: fever ≥38.3°C, severe symptoms, peritoneal signs, tubo-ovarian complex/abscess, immunocompromised, pregnancy, IUD in place, or failed outpatient therapy → consider hospitalization

caption: Treatment Regimens for Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
rows: Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM once + Azithromycin 1 g PO once, then Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD
Cefixime 400 mg PO once + Metronidazole 500 mg PO BD + Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD
Ceftriaxone 1 g IV Q8H + Doxycycline 100 mg IV/PO BD ± Metronidazole 500 mg IV Q8H
Until clinical improvement, then switch to oral x 7 days total

text: Genital ulcer disease (GUD) presents with ulcers in the genital area. Causes vary by geography: Haemophilus ducreyi (chancroid) in Africa, Klebsiella granulomatis (granuloma inguinale) in tropics, Chlamydia trachomatis L1-L3 (lymphogranuloma venereum/LGV) in Asia/Africa, Treponema pallidum (syphilis) globally, Herpes simplex virus (HSV) globally.

caption: Syndromic Treatment Regimens for Genital Ulcer Disease
rows: Azithromycin 1 g PO single dose OR Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD x 3 days OR Erythromycin 500 mg PO QID x 7 days
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD x 21 days
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD x 21 days
Erythromycin 500 mg PO QID x 21 days OR Azithromycin 1 g weekly x 3 weeks
Penicillin G benzathine 2.4 million units IM single dose
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD x 28 days

text: Inguinal lymphadenopathy can be STI-related (buboes from chancroid, LGV) or non-STI (tuberculosis, filariasis, lymphoma). Buboes are painful, suppurative inguinal lymph nodes typically unilateral, developing 1-3 weeks after genital ulcer. Lymphadenopathy may occur without visible ulcer (especially LGV).

items: Treat presumptively for both chancroid and LGV: Azithromycin 1 g PO single dose for chancroid + Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD x 21 days for LGV
Do NOT incise/drain buboes — allows secondary infection and scar formation; manage expectantly with analgesics
Aspiration only if severe pain affecting function — use needle aspiration, NOT incision
Monitor for resolution; if not improving in 3-4 weeks, reassess diagnosis

text: Key actions: Treat all partners of STI-positive patients. Partners of patients with urethritis/cervicitis should receive empiric treatment for gonorrhea + chlamydia. Partners of PID patients should be evaluated and treated. Counsel all patients on condom use, sexual abstinence until treatment complete, and partner notification. Retest 3 months after treatment to detect reinfection.

Pregnant women: avoid doxycycline; use Azithromycin 1 g PO single dose for chlamydia, Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose for gonorrhea. Penicillin allergy: confirm type of reaction; if non-anaphylactic, cephalosporins generally safe (cross-reactivity ~1-3%).
